before: Dizzy Gillespie and the Double Six of Paris is a 1963 studio album collaboration between Dizzy Gillespie and Les Double Six, also known as the Double Six of Paris, a French vocal group who sings in vocalese to songs associated with Dizzy Gillespie. Gillespie, pianist Bud Powell, and a rhythm section accompany; two of the songs feature his quintet, with James Moody. It was reissued on CD in 1989.
after: A Jazz Hour With Dizzy Gillespie is a 1992 Dizzy Gillespie collection issued on Distribution Movieplay. It gathers bebop and Latin-jazz staples associated with the trumpeter, including "Manteca," "Groovin' High," "Tin Tin Deo," and "Birk's Works," alongside standards such as "Alone Together" and "I Can't Get Started."
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release title, date (1992), label (Distribution Movieplay), artist, tracklist (Manteca, Groovin' High, Tin Tin Deo, Birk's Works, Alone Together, I Can't Get Started)

before: "Soul Makossa" is a song by Cameroonian saxophonist and songwriter Manu Dibango, released as a single in 1972. It is the most sampled African song in history. The song was originally recorded as the B-side for "Hymne de la 8e Coupe d'Afrique des Nations", a song celebrating the Cameroon national football team's accession to the quarterfinals of the Africa Cup of Nations football tournament, as well as Cameroon's hosting the games for the first time; the lyrics were written by Cameroonian poet and musicologist S.M. Eno Belinga. Except for some words in English, it was written in Duala, a native dialect continuum from Cameroon. A makossa-funk landmark and one of the most influential African records in global pop. Michael Jackson's 'Wanna Be Startin' Somethin'' borrowed its chant, creating one of music's first copyright disputes.
after: Soul Makossa is a 1973 album by Cameroonian saxophonist Manu Dibango. Built around the title track and "Lily," the record runs through Dibango's makossa-funk with tracks including "New Bell," "Dangwa," and "Hibiscus."
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release title, date (1973), artist; ours year/label; tracklist (Soul Makossa, Lily, New Bell, Dangwa, Hibiscus). Wiki extract ignored (song entity, wikipedia=no)

before: "Ain't Misbehavin'" is a 1929 stride jazz/early swing song. Andy Razaf wrote the lyrics to a score by Thomas "Fats" Waller and Harry Brooks for the Broadway musical comedy play Connie's Hot Chocolates. It was published by Mills Music.
after: Ain't Misbehavin' is a Fats Waller collection named for his signature stride number. It pulls together his piano-and-vocal sides, including "Ain't Misbehavin'," "Honeysuckle Rose," "Your Feet's Too Big," "Two Sleepy People," and "The Sheik of Araby."

before: Aquarius is an album by American jazz flautist Nicole Mitchell, which was recorded in 2012 and released on Delmark. It was the debut of her ensemble Ice Crystal, a quartet with vibraphonist Jason Adasiewicz, bassist Josh Abrams and drummer Frank Rosaly. The last piece is a tribute to saxophonist Fred Anderson.
after: All Things Are is a 2019 album credited to the Matthew Shipp Trio with flautist Nicole Mitchell, released on RogueArt. The program of mostly elemental titles includes "Elements," "Well Spring," "Hidden Essence," "Void of Ground," and the closing "All Things Are."

before: Haydn, Hummel, L. Mozart: Trumpet Concertos is a studio album of trumpet concertos by Joseph Haydn, Leopold Mozart and Johann Nepomuk Hummel, performed by Wynton Marsalis with the National Philharmonic Orchestra under the direction of Raymond Leppard. The album won a Grammy award in 1984 for Best Instrumental Soloist Performance with orchestra.
after: Baroque Music for Trumpets is a 1984 classical album by trumpeter Wynton Marsalis with the English Chamber Orchestra under Raymond Leppard, with soprano Edita Gruberova, on CBS Records Masterworks. The baroque program includes a Concerto in D major for trumpet, oboes and strings, "Let the Bright Seraphim" from Samson, and "Sound the Trumpet" from Come Ye Sons of Art.

before: Harold Melvin & the Blue Notes were an American soul and R&B vocal group. One of the most popular Philadelphia soul groups of the 1970s, the group's repertoire included soul, R&B, doo-wop, and disco. Founded in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, in the middle of the 1950s as The Charlemagnes, the group is most noted for several hits on Gamble and Huff's Philadelphia International label between 1972 and 1976, although they performed and recorded until Melvin's death in 1997. Despite group founder and original lead singer Harold Melvin's top billing, the Blue Notes' most famous member was Teddy Pendergrass, their lead singer during the successful years at Philadelphia International. Captured South African jazz at its peak before apartheid forced musicians into exile. The Blue Notes became the nucleus of the London jazz scene.
after: Blue Notes in Concert is a 1964 album credited to The Blue Notes, released on Gallo.
evidenceUsed: ours title/artist/year/label only. MusicBrainz returned no release; wiki extract is the wrong entity (Harold Melvin & the Blue Notes soul group, wikipedia=no). No entity-matched evidence available

before: A 1997 Tudor date, Blue Rondo finds Charlie Parker in a bebop setting. Among the tracks are "Blue Rondo a la Turk" and "Première rhapsodie". The album comes from the contemporary era's stylistic eclecticism, drawing on the full history of jazz and beyond.
after: Blue Rondo is a 1997 release on Tudor whose program mixes jazz themes with clarinet recital repertoire. The tracklist ranges across "Blue Rondo à la Turk," Debussy's "Première rhapsodie," "Waltz for Debby," and a Clarinet Sonatina.

before: Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers is a studio album by Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers, released on September 1, 1961, through Impulse! Records. Expanding to a sextet for the first time, it was the group's final recording with Bobby Timmons, who would be replaced by Cedar Walton. Showed the Messengers' versatility and ability to transform standards into hard bop vehicles.
after: Caravan is an Art Blakey & the Jazz Messengers album on Riverside. The hard-bop date opens with the title tune and runs through "Sweet 'n' Sour," "This Is for Albert," "Skylark," and "In the Wee Small Hours of the Morning."

before: A Tale of God's Will (A Requiem for Katrina) is a studio album recorded in 2007 by the Terence Blanchard Quintet. The album was originally released on August 14, 2007 (2007-08-14) by Blue Note Records. A powerful statement about New Orleans' cultural importance and resilience. Blanchard's film scoring influence is evident.
after: Congo Square is a 2006 album by trumpeter Terence Blanchard, released on Blue Note.

before: Black Pearl is an album by the American jazz duo Harrison/Blanchard, released in 1988. It was their final album together; Terence Blanchard began his long partnership with the director Spike Lee on School Daze, released the same year. The duo supported the album with a North American tour.
after: Crystal Stair is a 1987 album co-led by trumpeter Terence Blanchard and saxophonist Donald Harrison, released on Columbia. It pairs standards such as "Softly as in a Morning Sunrise" and "God Bless the Child" with originals including the title track, "Endicott," and "Slam."

before: Double Vision is a 1986 album by Bob James and David Sanborn. The album was a successful smooth jazz release receiving frequent airplay. The original album was released in the US on May 19, 1986, by Warner Bros Records. It was released a week later in the UK.
after: David Sanborn is the self-titled album by alto saxophonist David Sanborn, released on Warner Bros. It includes a version of Paul Simon's "I Do It for Your Love" alongside "Indio," "Smile," "Mamacita," and "Concrete Boogie."

before: Jean Reinhardt, known by his Romani nickname Django, was a Belgian-born Romani jazz guitarist and composer who lived most of his life in France. He was one of the first major jazz talents to emerge in Europe and has been hailed as one of its most significant exponents. Invented European jazz. Django's influence extends from jazz to rock to countless guitarists who marvel at his impossible virtuosity.
after: Djangology is a collection of guitarist Django Reinhardt's gypsy-jazz recordings issued on Vogue. It gathers his signature sides, including "Djangology," "Minor Swing," "Nuages," and "Swing 39."

before: "Nuages" is one of the best-known compositions by Django Reinhardt. He recorded at least thirteen versions of the tune, which is a jazz standard and a mainstay of the gypsy swing repertoire. English and French lyrics have been added to the piece, which was originally an instrumental work. The title translated into English is "Clouds", but the adaptation with English lyrics is titled "It's the Bluest Kind of Blues".
after: Nuages is a collection of guitarist Django Reinhardt's recordings, named for his celebrated tune. The set runs through gypsy-jazz sides including "Nuages," "Minor Swing," "Swing Guitars," "Daphne," and "Belleville."
